text: The surgeon identifies the junction between the ureter and renal pelvis, noting the presence of a crossing vessel, which is the main artery and vein. The renal pelvis is mobilized superior to these vessels, with careful manipulation in both anterior and posterior positions.

The surgeon performs a diamond-shaped excision of the ureteropelvic junction (UPJ) after fully mobilizing the renal pelvis. The medial surface of the renal pelvis is carried to its superior aspect, followed by excision of tissue both anteriorly and posteriorly as part of the reduction pyeloplasty.

The surgeon begins closing the renal pelvis using a 3-0 V-Loc suture, creating self-retraction by hooking it to the perineum. The ureter is spatulated on the lateral aspect, and the UPJ holder area is prepared for excision. The anastomosis is initiated on the renal pelvis side using a fibroviral suture, focusing on the posterior aspect due to its complexity.

The surgeon places a stent in an antegrade fashion after partially completing the anastomosis on each side. The UPJ is excised and sent for pathology. Methylene blue is used to backfill the bladder, with a hint of blue visible, confirming the stent's placement. The danune hook is removed to complete the renal pelvis closure, and the area is retroperitonealized.
